title: Suit Warehouse

jokes.text: Jim and Dwight Schrupert. I was the dynamic, likeable winner that was doted upon by Mom. Whoo-hoo!
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining their fake family business approach
jokes.punchline: The absurd fake name 'Schrupert' and his delusional self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight delusion with a perfectly absurd fake name. Character-specific and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have created a fake family business. My alias is Schrupert Pupkenstein, a wealthy industrialist with connections in Eastern Europe.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I am excellent at deception. I have fooled everyone at Dunder Mifflin multiple times.

jokes.text: And Jim was the closeted foot fetishist pretending to belong.
jokes.setup: Continuing the fake family backstory
jokes.punchline: Completely unexpected and weirdly specific insult about Jim
jokes.explanation: Bizarrely specific and unexpected character assassination. Pure Dwight weirdness.

jokes.text: I hope you've been paying your wig storage bills, Jimbo, 'cause it's time for another episode of Handsome and Stinky, Paper Brothers for Hire.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to get Jim to do another fake family sale
jokes.punchline: 'Handsome and Stinky' as a duo name, obviously casting Jim as Stinky
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific duo name that shows Dwight's ego and Jim-insulting tendency.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: We need to do another fake family sale. You and I would make a great team.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: We could call ourselves 'Handsome and Stinky.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Which one am I?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Take a guess.

jokes.text: Phyllis, my sister? More like my dead great-great-grandmother who died of stupidity.
jokes.setup: Jim suggesting Phyllis as a replacement family member
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific and mean genealogical insult
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight meanness with absurd specificity. 'Died of stupidity' is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know what? Phyllis could be your replacement family member.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. Phyllis is a second cousin twice removed on her mother's side, which makes her genetically 3.125% related to me. That's not family, that's a statistical error.

jokes.text: Oh, do you really have ears, Phyllis? Like all human beings?
jokes.setup: Phyllis saying 'I have ears' to Dwight's insult
jokes.punchline: Dwight's condescending fake-surprise at basic human anatomy
jokes.explanation: Solid Dwight condescension but fairly predictable structure.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You have ears? I had no idea. Congratulations on that.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: I have ears.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh really? Well that's fascinating. Next you'll be telling me you have a nose.

jokes.text: You can make it in 30 minutes if you drive 240 miles per hour.
jokes.setup: Dwight demanding Jim come from Philadelphia immediately
jokes.punchline: Absurd speed calculation delivered seriously
jokes.explanation: Typical Dwight obliviousness to reality, but the joke is fairly straightforward.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim, you need to get back from Philadelphia immediately. I'm talking now.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, it's a three-hour drive.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Not if you drive 120 miles per hour. You'll be here in an hour and a half. I've done the math.

jokes.text: A hundred and eighty. Um, that comes to 25 minutes. Yes. Oh, well, thank you, Jim. Yes, I am better than you.
jokes.setup: Jim helping with math calculation remotely
jokes.punchline: Dwight taking credit for Jim's help and declaring superiority
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight ego moment - taking credit while simultaneously being helped.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, so you take the square root of 144...

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Which is 12. I knew that.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Right, and then you multiply by 3.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: 36. Obviously. My superior mathematical intellect has solved this problem.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, I just walked you through every step.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Exactly. And I mastered it instantly. That's the mark of a true genius.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Why didn't I think of this? Andy's not here.
jokes.setup: Darryl realizing he can leave with Jim
jokes.punchline: Sudden realization that the absent boss means freedom
jokes.explanation: Relatable workplace realization delivered with good timing.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... Michael's not here.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Jim, we can do whatever we want.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I mean, nobody's watching. We could leave right now and nobody would even know.

jokes.text: I haven't been trained for this.
jokes.setup: Erin being asked to handle a simple pen delivery
jokes.punchline: Treating pen reception as requiring specialized training
jokes.explanation: Pure Erin - overcomplicating the simplest task. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh my god, a pen delivery? I'm going to need to consult my manual. Do we have a manual for this? We should have a manual.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Okay, so first I need to assess the structural integrity of the box. Is it a ballpoint? Gel? This is important.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: And then I'll need to sign for it, initial it, date it, and perform a small blessing. It's just protocol.

jokes.text: It was like making love with a wild animal. But not like a cougar, like you might think. It was like a swarm of bees.
jokes.setup: Clark describing his encounter with Jan
jokes.punchline: Increasingly bizarre animal metaphors for intimacy
jokes.explanation: Genuinely unexpected metaphor progression from wild animal to swarm of bees.

jokes.text: Bees that just find something wrong with every hotel room.
jokes.setup: The bees metaphor from previous line
jokes.punchline: Adding mundane complaining behavior to the bee metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - takes the absurd bees metaphor and adds perfectly mundane hotel complaints.

jokes.text: Like my mom used to say, 'Talk classy. Act nasty.'
jokes.setup: Clark talking about being a gentleman
jokes.punchline: Meredith's mother's hilariously inappropriate wisdom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Meredith - inappropriate family wisdom delivered as if normal. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: My mother always said, 'Meredith, a gentleman is just a patient wolf.'

jokes.text: What? Is it on backwards?
jokes.setup: Someone asking about Meredith's wig
jokes.punchline: Her casual concern about wearing a wig backwards
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy setup with Meredith's characteristic casualness about her appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: What? It's not backwards. I checked in the mirror this morning.

jokes.text: Always with the friends, Oscar. Can't we just enjoy the new espresso machine?
jokes.setup: Oscar mentioning his friend has the same espresso machine
jokes.punchline: Angela's passive-aggressive complaint about Oscar always mentioning friends
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment - Angela's cattiness about Oscar's social references.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Does Oscar have any friends that he doesn't own the same exact thing as?

jokes.text: Actually, it's pronounced espresso. Wait, that's what you said. I apologize. I just assumed you would mispronounce it, so...
jokes.setup: Oscar trying to correct pronunciation
jokes.punchline: Realizing he's correcting correct pronunciation, awkwardly explaining his assumption
jokes.explanation: Perfect Oscar moment - his know-it-all tendency backfiring awkwardly.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: It's pronounced 'GIF', not 'JIF'.

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Actually, the creator said it's 'JIF'.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Oh. Well... I was just... I thought you were saying it wrong, so I was helping. You're welcome anyway.

jokes.text: If it will lead to me being a salesman, I'll pretend to be your friend.
jokes.setup: Dwight asking Clark to pretend to be his son
jokes.punchline: Clark's brutally honest admission about fake friendship for career advancement
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny honesty about workplace relationships and ambition.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Clark, I need you to pretend to be my son.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Dwight, I've been pretending to be your friend for three years to get ahead at this company. Pretending to be your son is just the next logical step.

jokes.text: I don't really have time to think about Pete right now. I just have a lot going on with this whole shipment of pens.
jokes.setup: Erin talking about being busy
jokes.punchline: Treating a simple pen delivery as overwhelming responsibility
jokes.explanation: Continues Erin's pen delivery anxiety storyline with characteristic overreaction.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm so busy! I have to deliver this pen to accounting, and then I have to... well, that's it, but it's a lot!

jokes.text: Really! You can't be scared of a room full of Jims. I love the guy, but he's basically Gumby with hair.
jokes.setup: Encouraging Darryl about his interview
jokes.punchline: Describing Jim as 'Gumby with hair' to make him seem non-threatening
jokes.explanation: Brilliant physical comedy description that's both loving and deflating. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: You're going to do great at your interview. Jim's really nice, he's not intimidating at all.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: He's like Gumby with hair.

jokes.text: He calls me Clarky 'cause he's my dad.
jokes.setup: Playing fake father-son with Dwight
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily explaining an obvious nickname
jokes.explanation: Simple cringe humor from overexplaining, but not particularly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Clark is short for... Clark.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, Dwight. That's how names work.

jokes.text: I mean, he's like a serial killer of animals. One time, he snuck up behind a sleeping deer and just sawed its head right off. It was sick. It was his birthday. Just turned three.
jokes.setup: Clark describing Dwight as a great hunter
jokes.punchline: Psychotic hunting story that reveals the deer was three years old (not Dwight)
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - the disturbing hunting story gets worse when you realize the deer's age, not Dwight's.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Dwight is a great hunter.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: One time he tracked a deer for three days straight. No food, no water, just him and the wilderness.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Finally he finds it. He raises his gun, looks into its eyes, and realizes it's only three years old.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: So he doesn't shoot it. He just... walks away.

jokes.text: Yeah, we're aware of what it means, Oscar. You just do not look cool saying it.
jokes.setup: Oscar explaining 'YOLO' means 'you only live once'
jokes.punchline: Jim's assessment that Oscar can't pull off the slang
jokes.explanation: Good character observation about Oscar's inability to use trendy slang naturally.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're not pulling that off, Oscar.

jokes.text: Man, the last time I talked months was, like, a million weeks ago.
jokes.setup: Jim talking about business timelines
jokes.punchline: Absurd time measurement mixing weeks and months nonsensically
jokes.explanation: Silly wordplay that doesn't quite land - feels forced rather than natural.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we're looking at about three weeks, maybe four months, possibly two weeks depending on how you count the months.

jokes.text: Jim, you've got a real Facebook energy going on here, man. You Zuckerberg'ed this place out.
jokes.setup: Darryl seeing Jim's startup office
jokes.punchline: Using 'Zuckerberg' as a verb for tech startup aesthetics
jokes.explanation: Decent contemporary reference humor, verbing Zuckerberg is mildly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: This place is so Zuckerberg.

jokes.text: Well, things between me and Clark are good, not great. In fact, I will say that they're not even good, really. They're bad. Like you and your son.
jokes.setup: Trying to relate to a client's father-son problems
jokes.punchline: Dwight's complete honesty about his fake relationship deteriorating
jokes.explanation: Great escalating honesty that undermines Dwight's own sales pitch.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My father and I had a very similar relationship. We never talked about our feelings. Of course, my relationship with my son is completely different. We're very close. He calls me every day.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Well, he did. Before he found out I was sleeping with his mother.

jokes.text: I sure was, just like he taught me. Looking cool.
jokes.setup: Dwight sarcastically asking if Clark was smoking marijuana
jokes.punchline: Clark playing along too earnestly with the sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe from Clark not understanding Dwight's sarcasm level.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Were you smoking marijuana?

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: No, no, of course not.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Right, because that would be really stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Yeah, it would be. I would never do that. That would be so dumb. I'm not that kind of person at all.

jokes.text: If this company is going down, I want to go down on it. With it, I want to go down with it.
jokes.setup: Darryl expressing loyalty to Jim's company
jokes.punchline: Accidentally sexual phrasing, then awkward correction
jokes.explanation: Standard accidental innuendo joke - functional but not particularly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I'm gonna stick with Jim's company no matter what. I'm gonna be there for him, you know? I'm gonna... I'm gonna be inside him.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm gonna be... working for him. I'm gonna work inside his company.

jokes.text: I don't want to be a busybody, but I don't want to be a lazybones. Busybody, lazybones, busybody, lazybones. Ah! My brain is ping-ponging around in my head right now. It's insane!
jokes.setup: Erin overthinking whether to unpack pens
jokes.punchline: Obsessive back-and-forth internal debate over trivial decision
jokes.explanation: Very Erin - overthinking simple decisions to an absurd degree. Character-specific neurosis.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Should I unpack the pens? I mean, they're already in the box. But they could be out of the box. Out is good. Out is accessible. But in is organized. In is... contained. What if I need a pen right now? I'd have to unpack them. But what if I unpack them and then realize I didn't need to? Then I've unpacked them for nothing. For nothing, Erin. Okay, I'm unpacking them. Wait, no. I'm leaving them. No, unpacking. Unpacking!

jokes.text: One! Two! Three! Kevin! Kevin! Kevin! Stop it!
jokes.setup: Presumably Kevin doing something disruptive during espresso tasting
jokes.punchline: Everyone having to physically restrain Kevin
jokes.explanation: Typical Kevin physical comedy - predictable but fits character.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: This one tastes like... burnt hair and regret.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Kevin, that's the dark roast. You're supposed to appreciate the notes.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: The only note I appreciate is a fifty dollar bill. This tastes like dirt.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kevin, it's a sophisticated blend—

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm making my own espresso. Where's the good stuff?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Step away from the espresso machine, Kevin.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: No! I know what I like, and I like it with three pumps of vanilla creamer and a donut chaser!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh my god, he's going for the industrial roaster—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Grab his arms!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've got his legs!

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: This is ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Let me go! I'm making cold brew! COLD BREW!

jokes.text: Oh, his last girlfriend was a transvestite. I knew it right away. Adam's apple like the prow of a ship. Thumbs like a lowland gorilla.
jokes.setup: Dwight insulting Clark's dating life to the client
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and bizarre physical descriptions
jokes.explanation: Dwight's bizarre specificity in insults, though the transvestite premise is dated humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Clark's dating life is a complete disaster. The man couldn't attract a woman if he were a magnet and she were made of iron.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: In fact, the last woman who showed interest in him had a lazy eye, a pronounced limp, and a collection of porcelain cats that numbered in the hundreds.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: She also insisted on wearing a fanny pack to formal events and had a PhD in marine biology, which she brought up constantly, despite living in Pennsylvania.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: She had webbed feet, which she tried to hide with special shoes, and she could only eat foods that were beige in color.

jokes.text: Like the time that you got drunk and killed those kids on their way to prom. That never happened. He's always been a liar.
jokes.setup: Clark threatening to reveal embarrassing Dwight stories
jokes.punchline: Absurdly dark fictional crime followed by Dwight's matter-of-fact denial
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Clark goes nuclear with fictional vehicular manslaughter, Dwight's casual denial is perfect.

jokes.text: Ever since he was a little kid, and he got caught saving 'treats' from the kitty litter box. Following the cat around on his knees with his hands cupped beneath its tail, going, 'Please, kitty, may I have some more?'
jokes.setup: Dwight retaliating with embarrassing Clark stories
jokes.punchline: Grotesquely specific story about eating cat feces with Oliver Twist reference
jokes.explanation: Absolutely disgusting and hilarious - the Oliver Twist reference elevates it to brilliant absurdity. Peak gross-out humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh, you think that's embarrassing? I've got stories about Clark that'll make your skin crawl. Like the time he ate cat feces thinking it was licorice, and I had to explain to him that Oliver Twist never actually asked for more of that particular dish.

jokes.text: He's got cat turd collector written all over him.
jokes.setup: Jim arriving, Dwight introducing him to the client
jokes.punchline: Immediately applying the cat feces story to Jim
jokes.explanation: Good callback that transfers the disgusting story to Jim immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: This is Jim Halpert, our top salesman. Jim, I was just telling Mr. Henderson here about the time I got cat feces on my face.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why would you tell him that?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: It's a story about perseverance. Jim here has also had cat feces on his face, haven't you Jim?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, Dwight, I have not.

jokes.text: Mandate. Always think of two men on a date.
jokes.setup: Business meeting using the word 'mandate'
jokes.punchline: Jim's juvenile word association
jokes.explanation: Basic juvenile wordplay - fits Jim's prankster side but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Did anyone else notice Michael kept saying 'mandate' in that meeting? Man... date.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm twelve.

jokes.text: I have gay friends. One gay friend. Oscar.
jokes.setup: Jim's talking head about the mandate comment
jokes.punchline: Correcting himself from plural to singular gay friend
jokes.explanation: Simple self-correction humor that shows Jim's honesty about his social circle.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I have a gay friend. Wait, I have gay friends. Plural. I don't know why I said that like it was surprising.

jokes.text: Hey, I find it helps if you just picture everybody naked.
jokes.setup: Trying to help nervous Darryl with interview
jokes.punchline: Classic terrible interview advice delivered earnestly
jokes.explanation: Tired cliché delivered without irony - weak joke that relies on familiar reference.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, so when you go in there, make sure you compliment the interviewer's tie.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Even if it's ugly. Especially if it's ugly.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And when they ask about your weaknesses, just say 'I'm too much of a perfectionist.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Everyone loves that one.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, and shake their hand really hard. Like, really hard. Show them you mean business.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: If you cry a little bit during the interview, that shows vulnerability and honesty.

jokes.text: When I say it out loud, I know that sounds insane, but it's the truth, I swear.
jokes.setup: Erin explaining her pen situation to Pete
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging how crazy her pen obsession sounds while defending it
jokes.explanation: Good self-awareness from Erin while maintaining her characteristic overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I know how this sounds, okay? I know I sound crazy. But these pens are important to me.

dialogue.speaker: Pete
dialogue.line: They're just pens.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: No, they're not just pens. They're my pens. And I need them to be a certain way.

jokes.text: Pam! Pam! Look out! Erin's gunning for your job!
jokes.setup: Erin handling pens/administrative tasks
jokes.punchline: Mock alarm about Erin's 'job threat' from pen duty
jokes.explanation: Mild teasing humor that plays off the pen storyline but nothing special.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm just organizing these pens by color and size.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: ALERT! We have a code red! Erin is consolidating our writing implements!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Dwight, she's just doing her job.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Her JOB? This is how it starts. Next thing you know, she'll be reorganizing the entire supply closet. We'll be obsolete!

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm really not trying to take anyone's job.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Too late. The pen rebellion has begun.

jokes.text: Well, that's funny, 'cause I think of him as working part-time in Philadelphia.
jokes.setup: Jim's coworker saying Jim works part-time in Scranton
jokes.punchline: Pam's contradictory perspective on Jim's work split
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing marital tension through different perspectives on Jim's time.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim doesn't work part-time. He works full-time here and full-time in Philadelphia. That's two full-times.

jokes.text: I think it would be like... You know what? I think it'd be like a Kevin Durant jump shot, perfecto.
jokes.setup: Darryl describing how he'd fit at Jim's company
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific basketball metaphor for job performance
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate sports metaphor but not particularly funny or clever.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I could fit in at Jim's company. I got the right attitude, the right work ethic. I'm like the point guard of the warehouse - I run the offense, I facilitate the game. At Attic Annex, I'd be the shooting guard, coming off the bench, ready to knock down threes when my number's called.

jokes.text: They're the ones who put a fish tank next to a basketball hoop.
jokes.setup: Context about Darryl accidentally killing fish while shooting baskets
jokes.punchline: Blaming the poor office design rather than Darryl's actions
jokes.explanation: Good logic about office design responsibility - economical and relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I mean, who designs an office with a basketball hoop right next to the fish tank? That's just asking for trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Exactly! It's not my fault I'm an amazing shot.

jokes.text: It's like, if I put a glass of milk on the edge of a table, and Cece knocks it over, I don't blame Cece. So, I'm like a three-year-old girl in this scenario?
jokes.setup: Pam's analogy about responsibility
jokes.punchline: Darryl's immediate recognition of the unflattering comparison
jokes.explanation: Good analogy humor with Darryl's quick recognition of being compared to a toddler.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: You know, having a baby is like having a plant. You have to water it, you have to give it sunlight, and you have to be responsible for it or it dies.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Did you just compare my baby to a plant?

jokes.text: Right? Thank you! It's just Philly. Everyone's acting like it's New York, or Paris, or London. Who needs it? Not us.
jokes.setup: Agreeing about not wanting to move to Philadelphia
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Philadelphia by comparing it unfavorably to major cities
jokes.explanation: Mild geographic snobbery humor - functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I mean, Philadelphia? It's not New York, it's not Los Angeles, it's not even Chicago.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's Philadelphia.

jokes.text: Yes! I love Philly! 215 or die! Come on, man!
jokes.setup: Just moments after dismissing Philadelphia
jokes.punchline: Complete 180-degree enthusiasm with area code reference
jokes.explanation: Good immediate reversal showing Darryl's opportunistic enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Philadelphia? Nah, man, that's not happening.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Did you say 215? That's my man's area code. I'm in. Let's go to Philly!

jokes.text: It feels really hot in here. Is it hot in here? It feels really, really hot in here. It's insane! They need to turn the AC on year-round. January too!
jokes.setup: Angela experiencing caffeine effects from espresso tasting
jokes.punchline: Increasingly frantic temperature complaints and irrational AC demands
jokes.explanation: Standard caffeine jitters humor - predictable but fits the setup.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Is it hot in here? Can someone turn down the air conditioning?

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: No, it's definitely too warm. I'm sweating. Turn it down more.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: It's still hot! Why is it so hot?! TURN IT DOWN!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: It's freezing now! Oh my God, it's freezing! Turn it UP! Who did this?!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Actually, it's too hot again. No wait—it's cold. It's both! How is it both?!

jokes.text: I don't get the point of this stupid window!
jokes.setup: Angela's continued caffeine-induced agitation
jokes.punchline: Irrational anger at basic office fixtures
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Angela's jitters - attacking windows is appropriately irrational.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: Why is this stapler even here? It's mocking me. Everything in this office is mocking me.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: The fluorescent lights, the beige walls, this chair that squeaks when I breathe — they're all conspiring against me!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: And don't even get me started on that water cooler. It gurgles. WHO PROGRAMMED IT TO GURGLE?

jokes.text: That's why his face always breaks out. Does that suit come with a fire extinguisher?
jokes.setup: Clark explaining his 'dad's' acrylic suit
jokes.punchline: Client's burn about the suit being a fire hazard
jokes.explanation: Solid burn about synthetic fabric flammability - good setup and payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: My dad's acrylic suit is perfectly fine. He's worn it for years.

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.line: Yeah, but isn't acrylic a fire hazard?

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: That's the beauty of it. If there's ever a fire, he'll melt right into the floor and escape through the cracks.

jokes.text: Yeah, you don't want Italian. You'll look like a mafia don. Next thing you know, you'll be doing life at Riker's Island.
jokes.setup: Discussing Italian suit options
jokes.punchline: Dwight's extreme stereotype about Italian fashion leading to prison
jokes.explanation: Typical Dwight extreme logic connecting clothing to criminal consequences.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Italian suits are the finest in the world. Tailored to perfection, constructed with precision, each stitch a work of art.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Of course, if you wear an Italian suit in Italy, you'll be arrested for impersonating the mafia. Then you'll go to an Italian prison, where they execute you with a fashionable blindfold.

jokes.text: That place doesn't exist. It's not a documented island. Cartograph much?
jokes.setup: Clark's insult about 'Boring Island'
jokes.punchline: Dwight's literal fact-checking and 'cartograph much?' comeback
jokes.explanation: Good Dwight pedantry with a clever 'much?' construction comeback.

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: You're from Boring Island.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Actually, Boring is a town in Oregon. It's not an island. Cartograph much?

jokes.text: Everyone, now that we have all this energy, why don't we move the copier into the annex like we've always wanted to?
jokes.setup: The office hyped up on espresso
jokes.punchline: Stanley suggesting productive use of caffeine energy for long-delayed task
jokes.explanation: Great Stanley moment - channeling unusual energy into practical office improvement.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: You know what? With all this energy, I could finally organize my desk. I've been putting that off for three years.

jokes.text: It's beautiful! Hardwood! I always knew it was down here. I just never dreamed that I would actually see it.
jokes.setup: Discovering hardwood floors under the carpet
jokes.punchline: Stanley's unexpectedly poetic appreciation of office flooring
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment - Stanley finding genuine wonder in office renovation.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: You know, I've worked in this office for years, and I never knew these floors were underneath. They're... they're beautiful. Like a fine wood grain, smooth as silk. I could look at this all day.

jokes.text: Killed their fish, and they still hired me! That's how you do that, baby!
jokes.setup: Darryl celebrating his job success
jokes.punchline: Taking credit for success despite (or because of) the fish incident
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reframes the disaster as somehow helping his case.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm taking credit for this success. All of it. The fish incident? That was strategic. That was me showing leadership.

jokes.text: Well, I can't stand here if you pull the carpet out from under me.
jokes.setup: Conflict about Kevin standing on carpet while others try to remove it
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'pulling the rug out from under someone'
jokes.explanation: Decent literal interpretation of common idiom - fits Kevin's literal-mindedness.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: What? I'm standing on it!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kevin, we need to remove the carpet.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Well, you're gonna have to pull it out from under me then.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's... that's literally what we're trying to do.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Yeah, so pull!

jokes.text: I saw the leaves twitch.
jokes.setup: Complaint about Creed pouring coffee in a plant
jokes.punchline: Creed's observation that the plant reacted to the coffee
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed - casually noting plant suffering with characteristic detachment.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: It needed the caffeine. You could see it perk right up.

jokes.text: And you closed it. The boys are back in town!
jokes.setup: Celebrating Dwight and Clark's successful sale
jokes.punchline: Jim's enthusiastic reference to classic rock song
jokes.explanation: Generic celebration reference - doesn't add much beyond filling the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We are the champions, my friends!

jokes.text: You got me! I used to collect them. Why? Each one is very different, like a snowflake.
jokes.setup: Jim asking if the cat turd story was really about Clark
jokes.punchline: Clark's admission and poetic justification for collecting cat feces
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback - the snowflake comparison elevates the gross-out humor to absurd poetry. Incredibly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, so that story was actually about you?

dialogue.speaker: Clark
dialogue.line: Yeah. Look, I collect cat feces. It's not something I'm proud of, but there's a poetry to it.

jokes.text: You left me in charge of the pens, Pam. That's what happened. The pens happened!
jokes.setup: Pam asking what happened to the office
jokes.punchline: Erin blaming the office chaos on pen responsibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect culmination of Erin's pen storyline - 'The pens happened!' is beautifully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What happened to the office?

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Well, if people would just take responsibility for their pens, none of this would have happened.

tmdb_overview: Clark brings an espresso maker to the office. Dwight and Clark pretend to be a father-and-son to land a sale. Darryl goes on an interview at Jim's company, where Pam drops by unexpectedly.
insight: Late-season slump: 75 score driven by character beats, not structural laughs.
